name: SKILL.md
content:
---
name: panelproof
description: >-
  PanelProof — a READ-ONLY proofer for Adobe Illustrator artwork. It checks finished files against
  a source of truth and tells you, field by field, what matches and what doesn't — it never edits
  anything. It is the proofing companion to Illustrator Data Merge: Data Merge builds the files,
  PanelProof verifies them. Two layers: a CONTENT proof that reads the live text out of .ai files
  and live-text PDFs and compares each value exactly to the spec, and a VISUAL proof that renders
  every file and reads the render — which is how it verifies outlined or rasterized PDFs that have
  no readable text, and how it catches a substituted font, text overset past its box, or a shifted
  element. Verdicts are four-tier: pass, present (found but confirm placement), review (must be
  checked on the render), fail (value not found). Use it whenever someone wants to check, verify,
  QC, proof, or "second-set-of-eyes" artwork against known values: nutrition / supplement / drug
  facts panels, back labels, spec sheets, price tags, certificates, any "does the artwork actually
  say what it's supposed to say" job. Trigger on "proof these", "check these labels against the
  spec", "did the panels come out right", "QC this against the spec sheets", "verify these files",
  "compare the artwork to the data". Use it even if they never say "PanelProof". It checks; it does
  not fix — fixing is Data Merge's job.
---

# PanelProof

> **v1.0.0** — Read-only proofer: check finished Illustrator artwork against a source of truth.

PanelProof is the verification half of the pair with Illustrator Data Merge. Data Merge stamps out
the files; PanelProof opens them back up and proves they say what they should. It **never edits or
saves artwork** — it reports, and reporting is the whole job. A proofer that also fixes is one
nobody trusts.

It checks in two layers, each playing to its strength:

- **Content proof (deterministic).** Reads the live text out of `.ai` files and live-text PDFs and
  compares every field to the spec, exactly. This is the layer you ship a label on.
- **Visual proof (the reader).** Renders every file and *looks* at the render. This is how it
  verifies outlined or rasterized PDFs that have no readable text, and how it catches the things
  text can't see: a substituted font, text overset past its frame, a shifted or missing element.

## What people ask for (you don't need magic words)

- "Proof these 11 panels against the spec sheets."
- "Did the labels come out right? Check them against this data."
- "QC this folder of artwork before it goes to the printer."
- "Verify the final PDFs match what we approved."
- "Second set of eyes on these — do the numbers match the spec?"

If the job is *artwork versus a known source of truth,* it's a proof.

## The four verdicts

Per field, and rolled up per file:

- **pass** — the mapped field matches the spec exactly (after light normalization of quotes,
  whitespace, micro/dash variants).
- **present** — the value isn't an exact field match but it *does* appear in the file's text
  (common across formats, or when a PDF reflowed the frames). The value is there; placement should
  be confirmed on the render.
- **review** — can't be checked by the content layer: the file has **no live text** (outlined or
  rasterized), there's no spec value for that field, or it's an image. The render is the proof
  surface — look at it.
- **fail** — the spec value is **not found** in the artwork at all. Shown with expected-vs-found.

The rule that keeps it trustworthy: **nothing that can't be verified is ever shown as a clean
pass.** No-live-text files are honestly marked `review` and resolved by reading the render, not
rubber-stamped.

## Safety mental model

The mechanical work is a vetted, **read-only** Illustrator engine (`scripts/panelproof.jsx`): it
opens each file, counts live text frames, reads the profile-mapped fields, captures the full
document text, renders a PNG, and closes **without saving**. The verdict logic lives in
`scripts/proofreport.js`. Your job is orchestration: resolve the one source of truth, assemble the
expected values, run the engine, then **read the renders** to resolve every `review` and confirm
every `present`. The visual read is done by you (the model) looking at the rendered PNGs — that is
the visual layer.

## Where it lives (deployed on the Mac)

`~/.panelproof/`: `panelproof.jsx` (read-only engine, reads `job.jsxinc` → writes `result.json`),
`proofreport.js` (verdicts → `report.html` + stdout summary), `run.sh` (timeout-wrapped runner).
No snapshot script — there is nothing to back up, because nothing is edited. Canonical copies live
in the skill's `scripts/`; redeploy from there if `~/.panelproof/` is missing (see
`references/install.md`).

## The workflow

```
1. SOURCE OF TRUTH   resolve ONE authoritative dataset; show the user the table you'll proof against
2. PROFILE           reuse the SAME per-template profile Data Merge uses (frame map)  → references/proof-workflow.md
3. PROOF (engine)    read mapped fields + full text + render every file               → mode "proof"
4. VERDICTS          proofreport.js scores four tiers                                 → references/verdicts.md
5. VISUAL READ       look at the render for every review/present, and spot-check pass  → references/visual-layer.md
6. REPORT            hand over pass/fail with expected-vs-found and the proof images
```

One source of truth, not "folder vs folder." Resolve the spec once (typed values, a spec sheet, or
the dataset Data Merge built from) and check all artwork to it. Reusing Data Merge's profile means
build and proof can never disagree about which frame is "Sodium."

## Reading the renders (the visual layer)

For every file the engine renders a PNG into `<inputDir>/_proof_previews` (writable; Illustrator's
PNG export is sandboxed out of `/tmp`). Then **you look at each one** and:

- For **review** files (no live text): read the values off the render and confirm them against the
  spec. Report them as *visually verified*, not as exact — a render can be misread in a way live
  text can't, so the honesty matters.
- For **present** files: confirm the value sits in the right place.
- For **pass** files: a quick glance catches a substituted font, overset text, or a shifted element
  that the content match alone wouldn't reveal.

Treat `_proof_previews` as scratch; delete it before handing off.

## Operating principles

- **Read-only, always.** PanelProof never edits. If something's wrong, it's reported; fixing is
  Illustrator Data Merge's job.
- **One source of truth**, shown to the user before proofing.
- **Respect the source even when it looks wrong.** If the spec has no terminal period, artwork with
  no terminal period *passes*. Never "correct" toward an assumption (the Gouda ingredient line is
  the canonical example).
- **No false greens.** Anything unverifiable is `review`, resolved on the render.
- **Content exact, visual advisory.** The content layer is exact on live text; the visual layer is
  authoritative on appearance but advisory on any text it read off pixels.

## FDA self-check (built in, opt-in)

For facts panels, add `fdaCheck:true` to the job and PanelProof recomputes each %DV from the
printed amount (`%DV = round(amount / DV * 100)`) and flags any value that doesn't reconcile —
catching an error on the **spec sheet itself**, not just an art-vs-sheet mismatch. It runs on every
amount/%DV pair it can read and is reported as a separate **advisory** line per file; it never
changes a content verdict. Allergen / "Contains" consistency against the ingredient list is the
companion check (documented; the Clearance Lab domain is the system of record). See
`references/domain-nutrition-facts.md`.

## Honest limitations

- Reads live text exactly from `.ai` and live-text PDFs; **outlined/rasterized PDFs have no text**,
  so they're `review` and verified visually (advisory).
- Frame-index field mapping is exact for files built from the known template; for arbitrary PDFs
  the engine falls back to text-presence (`present`) and the render confirms placement.
- Illustrator must be open; fonts must be installed or a substitution is what you'll (correctly)
  see on the render.
- It checks; it does not fix.

## First-run setup

First time on a machine → `references/install.md`. A plain-English guide for non-technical
teammates ships as `panelproof-install-guide.html`.

name: assets/example-spec-nutrition.jsxinc
references/domain-nutrition-facts.md
content:
# Domain pack — Nutrition / Supplement / Drug Facts (proofing)

Reusable knowledge for proofing FDA facts panels. The general proofer doesn't know anything about
food; this pack supplies the field keys, the optional %DV cross-check, the allergen consistency
check, and the fidelity rules that decide pass vs. fail vs. respect-the-source.

## Field keys (match the Data Merge nutrition pack)

`cal`, `totfat`/`totfat_dv`, `satfat`/`satfat_dv`, `trans`, `chol`/`chol_dv`, `sodium`/`sodium_dv`,
`carb`/`carb_dv`, `fiber`/`fiber_dv`, `sugars`, `addsugars`/`addsugars_dv`, `protein`,
`vitd`/`vitd_dv`, `calcium`/`calcium_dv`, `iron`/`iron_dv`, `potassium`/`potassium_dv`, `serving`,
`servings`, `ingredients`, `allergens`. For combined frames the expected value is the whole frame
content (`"Saturated Fat 5g"`, `"Calcium 198mg"`, `"Vitamin D 0mcg"`, `"Potassium 34mg"`).

## Optional cross-check 1 — recompute %DV (built in via `fdaCheck:true`)

Set `fdaCheck:true` in the job to turn this on. It catches an error on the spec sheet itself, not just a transcription slip. `%DV = round(amount / DV
* 100)`. Reference DVs (adults & children ≥4):

| nutrient | DV | nutrient | DV |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Fat | 78 g | Added Sugars | 50 g |
| Saturated Fat | 20 g | Vitamin D | 20 mcg |
| Cholesterol | 300 mg | Calcium | 1300 mg |
| Sodium | 2300 mg | Iron | 18 mg |
| Total Carbohydrate | 275 g | Potassium | 4700 mg |
| Dietary Fiber | 28 g | | |

If a printed %DV disagrees with the recomputed value by more than rounding, flag it for human
review — report it, never "fix" it.

## Optional cross-check 2 — allergen / "Contains" consistency

The allergen / "Contains" statement must be consistent with the ingredient list. If the ingredients
name milk but the allergen line omits it (or vice versa), flag it. This is the Clearance Lab
domain; PanelProof flags, Clearance Lab is the system of record for compliance.

## Fidelity rules (decide the verdict honestly)

- **Ingredients: exact.** Casing, commas, terminal punctuation all matter. If the spec line ends
  with no period, artwork with no period **passes** — do not fail it toward an assumed period.
  (Gouda's no-period line is the canonical example; it passed against its sheet.)
- **Colored vs white variants.** "ANNATTO VEGETABLE COLOR (IF COLORED)" may legitimately cover both
  a colored and a white SKU with one identical panel. Proof each file against the spec it should
  carry; don't assume a white product drops the colorant unless its sheet says so.
- **Added ingredients.** A "with peppers" SKU has a different ingredient line and may differ on
  nutrition; proof it against its own sheet, not a sibling's.
- **Identical numbers across products are fine** — proof each against its own expected record.

## Format note

Final facts-panel PDFs frequently ship with **outlined type**. Those have no live text, so the
content layer marks every field `review` and the visual layer verifies them off the render (see
`references/visual-layer.md`). That's the expected, correct path for outlined art — not a failure.

name: references/install.md
content:
# Install / first-run setup

PanelProof runs a small **read-only** engine from `~/.panelproof/`. One-time setup per machine. A
plain-English version ships as `panelproof-install-guide.html` for non-technical teammates.

## 1. Deploy the engine

```bash
mkdir -p ~/.panelproof/dist
cp scripts/panelproof.jsx scripts/run.sh scripts/proofreport.js ~/.panelproof/
chmod +x ~/.panelproof/run.sh
```

Requires Adobe Illustrator, Node (for the report), and zsh. There is **no snapshot script** — the
engine never edits artwork, so there is nothing to back up.

## 2. Illustrator must be open

No headless mode. The runner wraps the call in `with timeout of 1800 seconds` so long batches don't
hit the ~120s Apple-event cap.

## 3. Automation permission

First run triggers macOS Automation permission ("… wants to control Adobe Illustrator"). Approve
it. If a run returns nothing, check System Settings → Privacy & Security → Automation.

## 4. Folder access (Cowork / Claude Code)

Grant access to the artwork folder and wherever the source-of-truth data lives.

## 5. The /tmp render gotcha

Illustrator's PNG export writes nothing to `/tmp`. Renders go to `<inputDir>/_proof_previews` (or a
`renderDir` you set). Treat that folder as scratch and delete it before handing off deliverables.

## 6. Smoke test

```js
// ~/.panelproof/job.jsxinc — discover builds/confirms a profile (same as Data Merge)
var PANELPROOF_JOB = { mode:"discover", template:"/abs/path/AnyTemplate.ai" };
```

```bash
zsh ~/.panelproof/run.sh
```

Then a real proof: point `mode:"proof"` at a folder with a small `expected` map and confirm the
report shows per-field verdicts and render thumbnails.

## Where things live

| path | what |
|------|------|
| `~/.panelproof/panelproof.jsx` | read-only engine |
| `~/.panelproof/proofreport.js` | verdicts → report.html + stdout summary |
| `~/.panelproof/run.sh` | timeout-wrapped runner |
| `~/.panelproof/job.jsxinc` | current job (you write this) |
| `~/.panelproof/result.json` | last extraction (engine writes) |
| `~/.panelproof/report.html` | last report (auto-opened) |

## Relationship to Illustrator Data Merge

PanelProof reuses Data Merge's per-template **profile** (the `key → frame` map). Keep them in sync:
the same profile that built a file should proof it. Data Merge writes; PanelProof verifies; neither
overlaps the other's job.

name: references/proof-workflow.md
content:
# Proof workflow & job schema

PanelProof reads `~/.panelproof/job.jsxinc` (global `PANELPROOF_JOB`, plain evaluable JS) and the
engine writes `~/.panelproof/result.json`. `proofreport.js` turns that into verdicts +
`report.html`. The engine is **read-only** — it opens, reads, renders, and closes without saving.

## Step 1 — Resolve ONE source of truth

A proof is artwork vs. a single authoritative dataset, never "folder vs folder." The source can be
typed values, a spec sheet (extract the values), or the exact dataset Data Merge built from.
**Show the user the table you'll proof against before running.** That eyeball is the cheapest error
check there is, and it makes the proof auditable.

## Step 2 — Reuse the Data Merge profile

Use the **same** per-template profile (the `key → frame` map) that Illustrator Data Merge uses for
this template. Sharing it means build and proof can't disagree about which frame is which field. If
there's no profile yet, run `mode:"discover"` (identical to Data Merge) to build one.

## Step 3 — Write the proof job

```js
var PANELPROOF_JOB = {
  mode: "proof",
  inputDir: "/abs/folder/of/artwork",   // omit / "" to use absolute paths in files[]
  render: true,                          // render every file for the visual layer (default true)
  renderScale: 300,                      // export percent
  renderDir: "",                         // default <inputDir>/_proof_previews (writable; NOT /tmp)

  profile: { fields: [                   // the shared frame map (key + frame index)
    { key:"cal", frame:64 }, { key:"sodium", frame:36 }, { key:"ingredients", frame:69 }
    // ...
  ]},

  files: [ "Gouda.ai", "Colby.ai" ],     // relative to inputDir, or absolute if inputDir is ""

  expected: {                            // the source of truth, per file, keyed by the files[] string
    "Gouda.ai": { cal:"100", sodium:"230mg", ingredients:"INGREDIENTS: PASTEURIZED MILK, ..." },
    "Colby.ai": { cal:"110", sodium:"170mg", ingredients:"INGREDIENTS: PASTEURIZED MILK, ..." }
  }
};
```

Notes:
- `expected` keys must exactly match the strings in `files`. For combined frames, the expected
  value is the whole frame's content (e.g. `"Saturated Fat 5g"`, `"Calcium 198mg"`), because the
  engine reads the frame's contents.
- To proof many products from one base with small differences, build the expected objects in JS
  with a clone helper (`function ext(b,o){var r={},k;for(k in b)r[k]=b[k];for(k in o)r[k]=o[k];return r;}`)
  — see `assets/example-spec-nutrition.jsxinc`.
- Mixed sources/formats: set `inputDir:""` and put absolute paths in `files` (and as `expected`
  keys) so `.ai` and outlined `.pdf` can be proofed in one run.

## Step 4 — Run

```bash
zsh ~/.panelproof/run.sh
```

Runs the engine in Illustrator (wrapped in `with timeout of 1800 seconds`), then `proofreport.js`,
then opens `report.html`. The stdout summary lists every file's status and each fail/present row,
which is what you relay.

## Step 5 — Read the renders

Open the report and look at the render for every `review` and `present` file (and glance at the
`pass` ones). Resolve `review` files by reading the values off the render and confirming against the
spec; mark them visually verified. See `references/visual-layer.md`.

## Result schema (what the engine writes)

```js
{ ok:true, mode:"proof", ts:"...", inputDir:"...", renderDir:"...",
  profile:{...}, expected:{...},          // echoed so proofreport.js has actual + expected together
  products:[
    { file:"Gouda.ai", liveTextFrames:70, mapped:{ cal:"100", sodium:"230mg", ... },
      fullText:"...all text joined by \\n...", render:{ png:"/abs/_proof_previews/Gouda.png", exists:true } }
  ]
}
```

`result.json` is rewritten after each file, so a long run shows progress and never loses work.

name: references/verdicts.md
content:
# Verdicts

PanelProof scores every field into one of four tiers, then rolls them up per file. The logic lives
in `proofreport.js`. The governing rule: **nothing that can't be verified is ever shown as a clean
pass.**

## Normalization (applied before comparing)

Both the expected and the found text are normalized so cosmetic differences don't cause false
fails: curly quotes → straight, en/em dashes → hyphen, micro sign `µ` → Greek `μ`, and runs of
whitespace collapsed to a single space, ends trimmed. Nothing else is altered — casing and digits
are compared as-is.

## The four tiers (per field)

- **pass** — the mapped frame's contents equal the spec value after normalization. The strong,
  exact result. This is what you get for `.ai` files built from the known template.
- **present** — not an exact field match, but the spec value appears in the file's full text. Two
  common causes: a PDF reflowed the text into different frames (so the frame index doesn't line up),
  or the value legitimately lives in a different frame. The value is in the file; **placement is
  confirmed on the render**. Matching uses a digit-boundary test so a short value like `0g` is not
  matched inside `10g`.
- **review** — the content layer can't judge it: the file has **no live text** (outlined or
  rasterized), there is no spec value for that field, or it's an image field. Resolved by reading
  the render.
- **fail** — the spec value is not found anywhere in the artwork. Reported with expected-vs-found so
  the fix is obvious.

## Per-file status

- **PASS** — every field passed.
- **PRESENT** — no fails, but at least one field was `present` (confirm placement on the render).
- **REVIEW** — no fails, but the file needs the render to verify (typically no live text).
- **FAIL** — at least one field failed.
- **ERROR** — the file couldn't be opened/read (path wrong, corrupt).

## Why "present" exists

A flat pass/fail lies on cross-format jobs. When you proof a production PDF whose text reflowed,
the right values are all there but not at the template's frame indices. Calling that "fail" would
cry wolf; calling it "pass" would overclaim placement. `present` says exactly what's true: the
value is in the file, go confirm where it sits on the render. For `.ai` files from the known
template you'll essentially never see `present` — those are exact `pass`.

## What the report shows

Per file: the status pill, and a table of only the `fail` and `present` rows (field, expected,
found, verdict) so a clean file is quiet and a problem file is loud. Every file shows its render
thumbnail, and no-live-text files are flagged "verify on render." The stdout summary mirrors this
for quick relay.

## The honesty line

The content layer's `pass` is exact and trustworthy. Everything the **visual** layer concludes —
every `review` you resolve by reading a render, and the placement you confirm for `present` — is
authoritative on appearance but advisory on any text read off pixels. Report visual conclusions as
"visually verified," never as exact content matches. That distinction is what makes the green
checks mean something.

name: references/visual-layer.md
content:
# The visual layer

The visual layer is what makes PanelProof a real proofer instead of a text-diff. It exists because
the failures that actually bite at the label stage are invisible to a text check:

- a font **substituted** because it wasn't installed,
- text **overset** past its frame or clipped,
- an element **shifted, missing, or overlapping**,
- and the big one: **outlined or rasterized PDFs**, which have no live text to read at all (a great
  many final production PDFs ship with type converted to outlines).

## How it works

The engine renders every file to a PNG (any format Illustrator opens: `.ai`, `.pdf`, `.eps`,
`.svg`) into `<inputDir>/_proof_previews`. Illustrator's PNG export writes **nothing** to `/tmp`
(sandboxed, silent), so renders always go under the input/output folder. Then **you (the model)
look at each render.** That visual read is the layer — not OCR, not a heuristic. Looking at the
rendered panel and reading it is more reliable than OCR for understanding a facts panel, and it
doubles as a layout check.

## What to do with each render

- **review files (no live text).** Read every spec-relevant value off the render and confirm it
  against the source. This is how an outlined PDF gets verified. Report it as **visually verified**,
  not as an exact content match — a render can be misread in a way live text cannot, so name the
  difference.
- **present files.** Confirm the value sits in the correct field/position (the content layer
  already found the value; the render confirms placement).
- **pass files.** A quick glance per file catches a substituted font, overset text, or a shifted
  element that an exact content match wouldn't reveal. Cheap insurance.

## Deterministic reference compare (optional add-on)

When a **known-good reference render** exists (a previously approved version, or the source `.ai`
render of a PDF you're checking), a pixel-diff is the deterministic complement to the visual read:
it answers "does this production PDF actually match its source art?" and flags drift, font
substitution, and shifts precisely. v1 ships the visual read as the core (dependency-free); a
pixel-diff helper can be added with Pillow when references are available. Keep it advisory and
region-highlighted, never a silent pass.

## Honesty

Content-layer `pass` is exact. Everything the visual layer concludes is authoritative on
**appearance** and advisory on any **text** it read off pixels. Always label visual conclusions
accordingly. The whole point of the four-tier system is that a green check never overclaims — the
visual layer extends coverage to files the content layer can't read, without pretending that
coverage is the same kind of exact.

name: scripts/panelproof.jsx
scripts/proofreport.js
scripts/run.sh
content:
#!/bin/zsh
# PanelProof runner: run the read-only engine in Illustrator with a long Apple-event timeout,
# then compute verdicts + open the report. Job is read from ~/.panelproof/job.jsxinc.
PP="$HOME/.panelproof"
osascript <<OSA
with timeout of 1800 seconds
  tell application "Adobe Illustrator" to do javascript (read POSIX file "$PP/panelproof.jsx")
end timeout
OSA
node "$PP/proofreport.js"
open "$PP/report.html" 2>/dev/null
